methodsAttempted.query: site:liverpool.ac.uk University of Liverpool AI data protection security Microsoft Copilot generative AI policies guidance
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search located the central policies and guidance hub, legal/security/data-protection page, and the Foundations of AI page describing Microsoft Copilot.
Followed official AI at Liverpool links among policies and guidance, golden rules, learning/teaching/assessment, legal/security/data protection, foundations, and research guidance.

artifacts.summary: Official-source discovery found a central University of Liverpool AI policy and guidance area with verified pages for golden rules, learning/teaching/assessment, legal/security/data protection, Microsoft Copilot, and research guidance. The older student news article and repository research poster were documented but rejected for current staged claims because they are not the central current policy/guidance pages.

artifacts.claimText: The University of Liverpool has a central AI policies and guidance hub that collates generative AI guidance covering golden rules, legal/security/data protection, learning/teaching/assessment, and research.

artifacts.claimText: Liverpool's central GenAI learning, teaching, and assessment page says the University has devised guidance to help academics and students understand its position and make informed decisions on when and how to use GenAI.

citation.citationTitle: University guidance on GenAI in learning, teaching and assessment
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.claimText: Liverpool lists unacceptable uses of generative AI in education, including unreviewed copying of AI-generated content into assessment materials or feedback, using AI for grades or final assessment decisions, uploading protected student or confidential information to public AI platforms without required review, and creating or modifying official policy documents without appropriate review and approval.

citation.citationTitle: University guidance on GenAI in learning, teaching and assessment
citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.claimText: Liverpool's legal, security, and data-protection guidance says GenAI use must comply with UK GDPR and that personal information should not be uploaded or shared with AI tools unless necessary and appropriate safeguards are in place.

artifacts.claimText: For University work, Liverpool says staff and students should use Microsoft Copilot as the endorsed generative AI chat tool, provided through the University's Microsoft 365 environment with data governance and safeguards aligned to institutional requirements.

artifacts.claimText: Liverpool's golden rules frame GenAI as a support tool rather than a substitute, and include expectations to acknowledge AI use where required, protect personal and sensitive data, and verify facts.

artifacts.claimText: Liverpool's research guidance says the University supports responsible and ethical GAI use in research, while expecting researchers and professional services colleagues to apply critical judgement, maintain transparency, prioritize integrity, and remain accountable for research outputs and related materials.

artifacts.sourceTitle: University guidance on GenAI in learning, teaching and assessment
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Unacceptable uses of generative AI in education include copying and pasting AI-generated content directly into assessment briefs, marking rubrics, or feedback without review; using AI to generate grades or make final assessment decisions; uploading student work, personal data, or confidential information to public AI platforms without first completing a Data Protection Impact Assessment.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Unacceptable uses of generative AI in education

artifacts.sourceTitle: Legal, security and data protection
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Any use of generative AI must be compliant with UK General Data Protection Regulations (GDPR). No personal information should be uploaded or shared with any AI tool unless it is necessary, and appropriate safeguarding and protection measures are in place.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Golden rules
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Use AI to support your work, but do not copy-paste outputs into assessed work, research submissions, or official documents without review and attribution. Where required, acknowledge AI use in your work. Include the tool name, version, and date of use for transparency.
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: University guidance on generative artificial intelligence for research
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The University expects all researchers and professional services colleagues to apply critical judgement, maintain transparency, and prioritise integrity at all times. Users remain fully accountable for all research outputs, submissions, analyses, and assessment materials - regardless of whether AI tools were used in their preparation.

artifacts.notes: Approved as a source-status claim because the evidence is the official central AI policies and guidance hub and does not overstate the linked content.

artifacts.title: University of Liverpool AI policy staged crawl
artifacts.summary: Staged official-source crawl for the University of Liverpool found central AI guidance pages covering the policy hub, learning/teaching/assessment guidance, legal/security/data protection, Microsoft Copilot, golden rules, and research guidance. Claims are staged as agent-reviewed candidates only and are not canonical.
limitations: This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
Rejected sources include an older student news article and a repository research poster; neither was used to support current staged policy claims.
Claims are narrow restatements of official pages and remain staged candidates pending any human publication workflow.
